topic: film photography — found a great camera shop in Shinsekai

turns.content: found the best camera shop today. like i'm genuinely excited about this which is ridiculous because i went in for a roll of film and left two hours later
Two hours in a camera shop when you went in for one roll of film is a very specific kind of time warp. Where was this?

shinsekai, tucked behind one of the kushikatsu places on the main drag. i've walked past it like a hundred times and never noticed it. tiny storefront, hand-painted sign, the kind of place that looks like it's been there since the 70s. inside it's just floor to ceiling cameras and lenses and boxes of old film stock
That sounds incredible — those hidden shops in Shinsekai are a whole genre. Was it more of a vintage/secondhand camera place, or do they carry new stock too? And what made you stay for two hours?

mostly vintage and secondhand but they had some fresh portra and ektar too which is what i originally went in for. i stayed because the owner is this older guy, maybe 70s, and he just started talking about the cameras and honestly i could have listened to him all day. he had this whole wall of rangefinders and he was telling me the history of each one. like the provenance, who brought it in, what condition it was in when he got it

There's something really special about shopkeepers who know the story behind every item they sell — it turns a transaction into something more like visiting a museum with a personal guide. Did anything on the wall catch your eye, or were you being disciplined about just getting your film?

oh i was not disciplined at all. he showed me this olympus xa2 in near mint condition. gorgeous little thing, like the proportions are so perfect. it's a clamshell design from the early 80s, fits in your jacket pocket, and the lens is surprisingly sharp for something that small. the whole form factor is like an exercise in economy of design. i held it for way too long

The Olympus XA series is a great example of that — Yoshihisa Maitani designed them and they really are a masterclass in compact design. Every element serves a purpose, nothing wasted. The clamshell cover doubling as the lens cap is such an elegant solution.

Did you buy it, or are you in the dangerous "thinking about it" phase?

i'm in the very dangerous thinking about it phase. he's asking 18,000 yen which is honestly really fair for the condition. i just can't justify it right now because, you know, invoices are slow this month and buying cameras when you're waiting on payments feels irresponsible. but like. it's so beautiful

18,000 yen for a near-mint XA2 is genuinely a good price — those have been climbing in the secondhand market. The responsible answer is to wait until invoices clear. The real answer is that you're probably going to think about it every day until you either buy it or someone else does.

Is the shop the kind of place where things move fast, or does stuff sit for a while? That changes the urgency calculus.

honestly it seemed like stuff sits a while. it's not the kind of place that has a huge walk-in customer base, it's a hidden gem not a tourist trap. so it'll probably be there next month. which is what i'm telling myself to avoid going back tomorrow lol. but the whole experience reminded me why i love shooting film in the first place. there's something about the physicality of it, the weight of a camera, the grain of the film, the fact that you get 36 shots and every one costs money so you actually think before you press the shutter

That constraint-as-creativity thing is real — when every frame costs money and there's no undo button, you compose more carefully, you wait for the light, you actually see what's in front of you instead of firing off fifty shots and sorting later. It's a fundamentally different relationship with image-making.

Do you shoot regularly, or is it more of a when-the-mood-strikes thing?

when the mood strikes mostly. i'll go weeks without shooting and then i'll have a weekend where i burn through three rolls walking around namba or the old neighborhoods near tennoji. it's like a reset for my visual brain honestly. all day i'm working with vectors and pixels and perfect alignment and then i go out with a camera and everything is texture and light and happy accidents. it's like the opposite of my work which is probably why i need it

That makes a lot of sense as a creative counterbalance — the precision and control of digital design work versus the unpredictability and tactile quality of film. One is about eliminating variables, the other is about embracing them. Having both probably keeps you from burning out on either.

Sounds like you've found a new favorite shop too. Even if you don't buy the XA2 right now, having a spot like that to wander into when you need film or just want to look at beautiful objects is worth knowing about.
yeah for sure. i'm definitely going back. worst case i buy some more portra and chat with the owner about rangefinders for another hour. there are worse ways to spend an afternoon honestly
There really are. And if the XA2 is still sitting there next time you go in and your invoices have cleared, that's the universe giving you permission. Enjoy the film in the meantime.
